glossary.definition: The action of a group on itself (or a module) by conjugation. For unit quaternions, the adjoint action of q on a pure quaternion v is defined as Ad_q(v) = qvq^{-1}, which yields the double cover of SO(3).

A geometric representation of the pure state space of a two-level quantum system (qubit) as the unit 2-sphere S^2 in R^3. Points on the surface represent pure states; the north and south poles correspond to the computational basis states |0⟩ and |1⟩.
An algebra over a field in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse. By the Frobenius theorem, the only finite-dimensional real division algebras are R, C, and H (quaternions).

A covering map p: X → Y in which every point of Y has exactly two preimages in X. SU(2) is a double cover of SO(3): the covering map π: SU(2) → SO(3) has kernel {+I, −I}, corresponding to the two unit quaternions {+1, −1}.
The quaternion algebra H introduced by William Rowan Hamilton in 1843, generated by the imaginary units i, j, k satisfying i² = j² = k² = ijk = −1.
A group that is also a smooth manifold, with the group operations (multiplication and inversion) being smooth maps. SU(2), SO(3), and Sp(1) are all compact connected Lie groups.

The property of a multiplication operation in which a·b ≠ b·a in general. Quaternion multiplication is non-commutative: for quaternions q₁, q₂ not parallel in H, q₁q₂ ≠ q₂q₁. This property is central to the design of multi-qubit quaternionic circuits.
A quaternion with zero real part: v = b·i + c·j + d·k ∈ H. Pure quaternions are identified with vectors in R^3 and form the three-dimensional subspace Im(H).

A hypercomplex number of the form q = a + b·i + c·j + d·k, where a, b, c, d are real numbers and i, j, k satisfy i² = j² = k² = ijk = −1. Quaternions form the algebra H, a four-dimensional non-commutative division algebra over R.

As defined in this paper: a unit quaternion q ∈ Sp(1), representing a pure state of a two-level quantum system via the identification Sp(1) ≅ SU(2). The quaternionic encoding provides a natural geometric picture of single-qubit operations as rotations in S^3.

The basic unit of quantum information: a two-level quantum system with state space the projective Hilbert space CP^1 ≅ S^2 (the Bloch sphere). Physically realized by spin-1/2 particles, photon polarization, and many other systems.

A mathematical object that transforms under a double-valued representation of the rotation group SO(3), i.e., under a representation of SU(2) that does not descend to SO(3). A spinor changes sign under a 2π rotation and returns to its original value under a 4π rotation. Quaternions provide a natural representation for spinors.
The compact Lie group of unit quaternions: Sp(1) = {q ∈ H : |q| = 1}. Topologically homeomorphic to the 3-sphere S^3. Isomorphic to SU(2) as a Lie group.

The special unitary group of degree 2: the group of 2×2 complex unitary matrices with determinant 1. It is a compact connected simply-connected Lie group, isomorphic to Sp(1) and topologically to S^3. The state space of a single qubit transforms under SU(2).
A quaternion q ∈ H with norm |q| = 1, i.e., a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2 = 1. Unit quaternions form the group Sp(1) ≅ SU(2) under quaternion multiplication.
